nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs at depths of approximately 1–25 meters; typically observed in pairs

decorPreferences: ample live rock for hiding and grazing

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding mainly on coral polyps along with a variety of benthic invertebrates. In captivity, a varied diet is generally advised — dried seaweed and Spirulina-based foods alongside vitamin-enriched mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped meaty marine foods. Multiple small feedings per day tend to improve long-term acclimation.

goodWith: mated conspecific (when introduced as an established pair)
avoidWith: additional conspecifics outside an established pair

summary: A Red Sea endemic butterflyfish with a pale-grey body, vertical black bars, and a distinctive white face crossed by a dark eye band. Generally observed in pairs on coral reefs; uncommon in the trade but reported to acclimate to captivity when housed in a mature, peaceful aquarium.
careNotes:
The White Faced Butterflyfish is endemic to the Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, and waters around Socotra, where it has been documented inhabiting coral reefs from roughly 1 to 25 m depth, typically in monogamous pairs. Although uncommon in the aquarium trade, it has been reported as not especially difficult to maintain in a well-established system with peaceful tankmates.

Cross-source disagreement was noted on several husbandry parameters and is documented here so reviewers can sanity-check:

- Adult size: LiveAquaria lists about 15 cm (6 inches); BlueZoo Aquatics lists about 12.7 cm (5 inches); FishBase records a maximum total length of 13 cm with a common length of 12 cm. The 12–13 cm range adopted here follows FishBase and Wikipedia.
- Care level: LiveAquaria rates this species 'Expert Only' while BlueZoo Aquatics rates it 'Intermediate' and Wikipedia states it is 'not a difficult species to keep'. The middle-of-the-range value of 'advanced' has been applied. A reviewer may consider downgrading to 'intermediate' if the omnivorous (non-obligate-corallivore) feeding profile is judged the dominant factor.
- Minimum tank size: LiveAquaria recommends 125 US gallons (~473 L); BlueZoo Aquatics recommends 75 US gallons (~284 L). Biasing toward the larger value as the tank-size rule advises, and accounting for the species' pair-bonded nature plus the need for ample swimming space and live-rock grazing area, the figure has been rounded to a nice value of 400 L (approximately 105 US gallons). Pairs in particular benefit from generous footprint.
- Temperature: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild-occurrence temperatures are 26.9–29.3°C (mean 28.3°C). The averaged 24–27°C range has been adopted; keepers may target the upper half (25–27°C) consistent with the Red Sea thermal environment.

This species is generally considered not reef-safe — it has been documented feeding on coral polyps and benthic invertebrates in the wild, and is best kept in a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system rather than a stony-coral reef. Conspecific aggression has been reported, so additional Chaetodon mesoleucos should only be added as a confirmed, simultaneously-introduced pair. Adequate live rock for shelter and grazing is generally advised, along with stable, well-aged water chemistry typical of mature reef systems.
breedingNotes: FishBase reports the species is oviparous and forms pairs during breeding. No established captive-breeding accounts in the aquarium hobby literature were located.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source — adult size, care level (Expert Only), reef-compatibility (No), minimum tank size 125 gal, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, omnivore diet guidance, conspecific aggression note.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked Red Sea native range, omnivore diet, not reef-safe; disagrees with LiveAquaria on adult size (5 in vs 6 in), minimum tank size (75 gal vs 125 gal), and care level (Intermediate vs Expert).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), maximum size (13 cm TL, common 12 cm), Western Indian Ocean distribution (Red Sea, Gulf of Aden), wild temperature range (26.9–29.3°C), depth range (5–20 m), and pair-bonding/oviparous reproduction.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution (central Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, Socotra), depth range (1–25 m), size (12–13 cm), reported diet (coral polyps and benthic invertebrates), pair association on the reef, and the note that the species is 'not difficult to keep in captivity'. Synonym Chaetodon hadjan recorded.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair-spawning oviparous species per FishBase; no established captive-breeding accounts known.
